varieties.name: Standard Clove (Syzygium aromaticum)
varieties.traits: Evergreen tree, 8-12m height, glossy oval leaves, small pale pink flowers clustered at branch tips
varieties.yield_notes: Zero to minimal in Brisbane climate. Trees begin flowering at 7-8 years (IF conditions met), full bearing at 15+ years. Brisbane's climate will prevent flowering.
varieties.availability: Rarely available in Australian nurseries. Some specialist tropical plant nurseries may carry seedlings. Not readily available in Brisbane due to climatic unsuitability.

varieties.name: 1. Zanzibar Type
varieties.origin: Originally from Zanzibar/Pemba Islands (Tanzania), introduced to India in 19th century, now widely cultivated in Kerala
varieties.traits: Smaller, slender buds compared to Penang type. Dark brown color when dried. Medium-sized tree with good branching habit.
varieties.yield_notes: High productivity - 2-8 kg/tree/year. Known for better adaptability and consistent yields.
varieties.availability: Widely available through Kerala Agricultural University, ICAR centers, certified nurseries in Kottayam and Kollam districts, Kerala State Spices Development Board.

varieties.name: 2. Penang Type
varieties.origin: Penang, Malaysia; introduced to India via spice trade routes
varieties.traits: Large, plump buds. Reddish-brown color. Vigorous tree growth.
varieties.yield_notes: Moderate productivity - 2-5 kg/tree/year. Prized for appearance and export quality.
varieties.availability: Available from specialized spice nurseries, Kerala Agricultural University research stations, limited commercial availability compared to Zanzibar.

varieties.name: 3. Amboyan Type
varieties.origin: Ambon Island, Maluku Islands, Indonesia (original native region of clove)
varieties.traits: Large, plump buds similar to Penang. Strong aromatic properties.
varieties.yield_notes: Slightly lower productivity than Zanzibar - 2-4 kg/tree/year.
varieties.availability: Limited availability in Kerala. May be sourced through specialized collectors or direct import of planting material from Indonesia.

varieties.name: 4. Burliar-1 Selection
varieties.origin: Selected from Burliar region, Nilgiris, Tamil Nadu (1850s cultivation zone). One of the first clove introductions to South India.
varieties.traits: High-yielding selection adapted to South Indian conditions. Medium-sized tree.
varieties.availability: Available from Tamil Nadu Agricultural University, Kerala Agricultural University, certified nurseries in Kerala-Tamil Nadu border regions.

varieties.name: 5. KC-1 (King Clove-1)
varieties.origin: Tamil Nadu selection, bolder flower bud variant identified in Kanyakumari district
varieties.traits: King type - larger, bolder flower buds than standard cloves. Robust tree structure.
varieties.yield_notes: Moderate - 2-5 kg/tree/year, but higher value per kg due to size.
varieties.availability: Tamil Nadu Agricultural University, Kanyakumari district nurseries, some Kerala nurseries near Tamil Nadu border.

varieties.name: 6. KC-2 (King Clove-2)
varieties.origin: Tamil Nadu selection, another bold bud variant from Kanyakumari
varieties.traits: Similar to KC-1, bold flower buds, slightly different tree morphology.

varieties.name: 7. LC-1 (Lilliput/Mini Clove)
varieties.origin: Tamil Nadu selection, normal/mini clove variant
varieties.traits: Smaller stature tree, smaller buds, spreading growth habit.
varieties.yield_notes: Lower weight per tree (1.5-3 kg/tree/year) but may be suitable for denser planting.
varieties.availability: Limited. Tamil Nadu nurseries, some experimental stations.

varieties.name: 8. Kanyakumari Clove (GI Tagged) 🏆
varieties.origin: Kanyakumari district, Tamil Nadu, Western Ghats. Introduced in early 1800s by East India Company at Courtallam. Geographical Indication (GI) tagged variety.
varieties.traits: Grown at 400-900m elevation in black soil rich in organic nutrients. High essential oil concentration. Characteristic aroma and rich flavor. Strong antioxidant properties.
varieties.yield_notes: 2-6 kg/tree/year. Kanyakumari district produces 80% of India's total clove production.
varieties.availability: Kanyakumari district nurseries, Kerala State Spices Development Board, certified through GI registry sources.

varieties.name: 9. Tuni Clove (Maluku Endemic) 🌏
varieties.origin: Native to Ambon Island, Maluku Islands, Indonesia. Ancient indigenous variety.
varieties.traits: Larger leaf, fruit, and seed morphology compared to other varieties. High eugenol content in buds (67.9%), stems (80.6%), and leaves (60.5%).
varieties.yield_notes: Variable in non-native conditions. 2-5 kg/tree/year if well-adapted.

varieties.name: 10. Afo Clove (Maluku Endemic) 🌏
varieties.origin: Native to Ternate Island, North Maluku Province, Indonesia. Ancient indigenous variety.
varieties.traits: Endemic Maluku variety with distinct morphological characteristics. Different from Tuni variety in tree structure and bud formation.

varieties.name: 11. Siputih Variety
varieties.origin: Hiri Island, Ternate, North Maluku, Indonesia. Local indigenous variety.
varieties.traits: Local adaptation to Hiri Island conditions.

varieties.name: 12. Posi-posi Variety
varieties.origin: Hiri Island, Ternate, North Maluku, Indonesia. Local indigenous variety.
varieties.traits: Local adaptation to Hiri Island conditions.

varieties.name: 13. Raja Clove (King Clove - Maluku)
varieties.origin: Native to Ambon Island, Maluku. Traditional 'king' type with distinctive morphology.
varieties.traits: Characterized by distinct morphological features specific to Ambon genetics.

varieties.name: 14. Forest Clove (Maluku Wild Type) 🌏
varieties.origin: Wild/forest populations in Ambon and Seram Islands, Moluccas. Ancient wild genetic stock.
varieties.traits: Wild type with natural variation. Characterized across 54 morpho-agronomical characteristics. Represents genetic diversity of original clove populations.
varieties.yield_notes: Variable, wild populations not selected for production.
varieties.availability: Not commercially available. Research/conservation germplasm only.

varieties.name: 15. Madagascar Clove Types
varieties.origin: Madagascar (introduced from Moluccas via French smuggling operations in 1770s). Madagascar is now world's leading clove exporter.
varieties.traits: Smaller buds than Sri Lankan types, stronger flavor suited for cooking. Categorized as CGI (Handpick), CG2 (Prima), CG3 (Courant). High oil content due to growing conditions.
varieties.yield_notes: Madagascar has very high productivity zones. Individual tree yields 3-8 kg/year.

varieties.name: 16. Sri Lankan Clove Types
varieties.origin: Sri Lanka (introduced historically through Arab traders or colonial powers). Cultivated in Kandyan Forest Gardens in Kandy, Kegalle, and Matale districts.
varieties.traits: Grown in mixed home gardens (Kandyan Forest Gardens) alongside pepper, nutmeg, and cinnamon. Larger buds than Madagascar types, milder flavor. Sun-dried processing.
varieties.yield_notes: Lower productivity - average 250 kg/hectare. Harvest October-January.
varieties.availability: Limited. Would require import from Sri Lankan sources or through regional spice networks. Kerala's proximity to Sri Lanka makes cultural exchange possible.

varieties.name: 17. Pemba Clove (Zanzibar Archipelago)
varieties.origin: Pemba Island, Zanzibar Archipelago, Tanzania. Some of the world's oldest clove trees. Introduced from Moluccas in early 1800s.
varieties.traits: Hand-picked at last moment before flowering for maximum size and mature flavor. Bright pink when ripe, turn brown as they dry. Significantly higher essential oil content (15-18%) than other world regions. Large cloves with strong aroma and intense taste. Used locally as natural anesthetic substitute.
varieties.yield_notes: Very high - up to 40 kg/tree/year for well-grown trees (exceptional trees up to 80 kg/year). Much higher than Indian averages.
varieties.availability: Very limited. Pemba cloves are premium export product. Planting material would require specialized import through spice research institutions.

varieties.name: 18. Sulawesi/Minahasa Clove (Indonesia)
varieties.origin: Sulawesi, particularly Minahasa region, Indonesia. Major Indonesian clove production area (Sulawesi produces 40%+ of Indonesian cloves).
varieties.traits: Strong, pungent flavor similar to Moluccas types. Valued in traditional spice blends.
